elements.text: Mid-day sun hangs like a dull copper coin behind a shroud of falling grey soot. Kaelen kneels in the warm ash, his fingers tracing the patterns of a thermal vent beneath a cracked shale shelf.
A few paces away, Renna crouches behind a rusted iron rib, her hand resting on the hilt of a jagged bone-shiv. Her eyes are narrowed, tracking his every movement.
The condensation rate of the sulfur-spring beneath this shale suggests a pocket of potable water every twelve hours. Exactly three inches below the frost-line.
Kaelen thrusts a thin metal spike into the grey crust, twisting it with calculated pressure. A soft hiss escapes, followed by a steady, thin trickle of clear, filtered moisture into a hollow reed.
You're digging in a dead zone, stranger. That water belongs to anyone with the teeth to keep it.
Renna steps out from the shadow of the rusted rib. She keeps her center of gravity low, her eyes darting between the trickle of water and Kaelen's throat.
It belongs to the one who understands the fluid dynamics of the shelf. Currently, that is me. I estimate you have three hours of hydration left before your motor skills begin to degrade.
I've lasted three years out here without a lecture on my kidney function. Give me the reed.
Kaelen does not pull back. He holds the reed steady, capping it with a wax-plug he scraped from a nearby ruin.

An inefficient exchange. If you kill me, you obtain half a liter of water and no knowledge of how to find the next deposit. If we cooperate, my calculations will increase your daily yield by forty percent.
Cooperate. Right. That's a word people use just before they slit your throat in your sleep.

I have no emotional investment in your demise. It would simply decrease my own survival probability. I require local geography, faction boundaries, and resource distribution. You require sustenance and a partner who does not rely on luck.
Renna stares at him, her gaze scanning his scarred face and the absolute stillness of his posture. She slowly relaxes her grip on the bone-shiv, though her stance remains defensive.
You speak like a machine. Or a corpse.
I am alive. And I intend to remain so.
Kaelen extends the reed flask toward her. She hesitates, then snatches it, taking a small, disciplined sip before pocketing it.
There's a camp two miles east. The Scrappers there don't negotiate. If you're as smart as you think you are, you'll help me avoid them.
Show me the path. I will calculate the safest route.
Kaelen rises, brushing the grey ash from his knees, his eyes already scanning the horizon for the next variable.
